items.q: How does 'der Mann' change in the accusative case?
items.why: Students think English-style: objects are unchanged. German changes ARTICLES based on case. Masculine is the only gender that visibly changes in accusative: der to den. Feminine, neuter, and plural look the same.

items.q: What case follows 'mit' (with)?
items.misconception: Accusative (because it sounds like a direct relationship).
items.why: Students guess case based on logic. Mit is a DATIVE preposition - one of several you memorise (aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu - mnemonic). Each preposition has a fixed case.

items.q: In 'Heute gehe ich ins Kino', why does 'gehe' come second?
items.correct: German is a V2 language - the conjugated verb is the second element in main clauses.
items.random_wrong: Heute is always followed by a verb.
items.why: Students apply English SVO. German fronts elements freely BUT the verb must be in second position. Subject moves if something else fronts: Heute (1) gehe (2) ich (3) ins Kino.

items.q: Where does the verb go after 'weil' (because)?
items.correct: At the end of the clause.
items.misconception: Second position, as in main clauses.
items.why: Students apply V2 everywhere. Subordinating conjunctions (weil, dass, wenn, ob, etc.) send the verb to the END: 'Ich bleibe, weil ich müde BIN' (not 'weil ich bin müde').

items.q: What is the gender of 'Mädchen' (girl)?
items.why: Students assume gender follows meaning. German gender is grammatical, not biological. The diminutive suffix -CHEN makes any noun neuter, regardless of natural gender. So das Mädchen, das Mädel.

items.q: Which words are capitalised in German?
items.correct: All nouns (and the first word of a sentence).
items.misconception: Only proper nouns, as in English.
items.random_wrong: Only the first word of a sentence.
items.why: Students apply English rules. German capitalises EVERY noun (Haus, Liebe, Auto, Bewegung). It's a strict rule and a marker of accurate writing - lowercase nouns instantly look wrong to natives.

items.q: Which auxiliary does 'gehen' (to go) take in the perfect tense?
items.why: Students default to haben (English 'have'). Verbs of motion or change of state take SEIN (gehen, kommen, laufen, werden). Most other verbs take haben. 'Ich habe gegangen' is incorrect.

items.q: How do you say 'I get up early' using 'aufstehen'?
items.why: Students keep separable verbs together. In present and simple past, the prefix SEPARATES and goes to the end: aufstehen becomes 'stehe...auf'. The prefix only rejoins in subordinate clauses, infinitives, and past participles.

items.q: What does 'Gift' mean in German?
items.why: A famous faux ami. German 'Gift' = poison; English 'gift' = das Geschenk. The same Germanic root diverged - in English it stayed as 'something given', in German it became specifically a poisonous gift.

items.q: Where does the main verb go in 'Ich kann gut Deutsch sprechen'?
items.correct: At the end of the clause (sprechen).
items.why: Students place the infinitive next to the modal. German modals push the main verb's INFINITIVE to the END: 'Ich kann gut Deutsch SPRECHEN' - the modal (kann) is in V2 position; the infinitive goes last.

items.q: What is the plural of 'Mann'?
items.why: Students use English -s plural. German has multiple plural patterns including UMLAUT + er for many masculine nouns: Mann to Männer, Buch to Bücher, Land to Länder. Plurals must often be memorised.

items.q: How do you say 'I will drink coffee tomorrow' in German?
items.why: Students translate 'will' as 'will'. The German 'will' (from wollen) means 'want', not future. Future = WERDEN + infinitive. Also, German often uses present + time word ('Ich trinke morgen Kaffee') for near future.

items.q: Where does 'nicht' go in 'I don't like the film' (Der Film gefällt mir nicht)?
items.correct: At the end (negating the whole sentence).
items.why: Students put 'nicht' near the start like English 'not'. German typically places sentence-negating nicht near the END. To negate a specific word, nicht goes immediately before that word.

items.q: What ending does an adjective take in 'der gute Mann' (the good man)?
items.why: Students apply noun gender to the adjective directly. Adjective endings depend on whether the article already shows case/gender. After der (which marks case), the adjective takes a weak -e.

items.q: How is the 'ch' in 'ich' pronounced?
items.correct: A soft palatal fricative (ich-laut), made at the front of the mouth.
items.why: Students substitute English 'ch'. German 'ch' has two sounds: ICH-laut (soft, front of mouth, after i/e/ä/ö/ü) and ACH-laut (hard, back of throat, after a/o/u). Neither matches English 'church'.

items.q: Why can you not just ignore umlauts in German?
items.correct: They change the pronunciation AND meaning of words.
items.random_wrong: They are pronounced the same as without dots.
items.why: Students treat umlauts as optional accents. They are CHANGED VOWELS. Schon (already) vs schön (beautiful) are different words. Mutter (mother) vs Mütter (mothers) are singular vs plural.

items.q: When does 'in' take the accusative vs dative?
items.correct: Accusative for movement INTO a place; dative for location IN a place.
items.misconception: It always takes the same case.
items.random_wrong: It depends on the speaker's mood.
items.why: Students don't realise two-way prepositions exist. Wechselpräpositionen change case by meaning: 'Ich gehe in die Stadt' (motion, accusative); 'Ich bin in der Stadt' (location, dative).

items.q: What does 'bald' mean in German?
items.why: Spelled identically to English 'bald' but means SOON. 'Bis bald!' = 'See you soon!'. The English 'bald' translates as 'kahl' or 'glatzköpfig'. Pure faux ami.

items.q: What case does 'helfen' (to help) require for its object?
items.why: Students assume direct objects are always accusative. Certain German verbs (helfen, danken, gratulieren, folgen, antworten) take DATIVE objects despite appearing transitive in English.

items.q: Which relative pronoun do you use for 'the man WHOM I saw' in German?
items.why: Students don't decline relative pronouns. The relative pronoun takes the case its FUNCTION REQUIRES in its clause. 'Whom I saw' makes it the direct object, so accusative: 'Der Mann, DEN ich gesehen habe.'

items.q: How do you say 'I would buy a car if I had money'?
items.correct: Ich würde ein Auto kaufen, wenn ich Geld HÄTTE.
items.misconception: Ich werde ein Auto kaufen, wenn ich Geld habe.
items.random_wrong: Ich kaufe ein Auto, wenn ich Geld hätte.
items.why: Students use future or present. Hypothetical conditions need KONJUNKTIV II: würde + infinitive (result) and hätte/wäre (condition). The condition uses subjunctive II, not present.

items.q: What does 'Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaft' mean broadly?
items.misconception: Three separate words run together by mistake.
items.random_wrong: It is too long to be a real word.
items.why: Students think long words are typos. German freely COMPOUNDS nouns into single words: Donau + Dampf + Schiff + Fahrt + Gesellschaft. The last element determines the gender and the core meaning.

items.q: What is the imperative 'du' form of 'machen' (to do)?
items.why: Students think you keep the present tense form. The du-imperative typically drops the -en or -st: 'Mach das!' (do it!), 'Komm!' (come!). The pronoun du is also normally omitted.

items.q: When addressing a stranger or someone older formally, which 'you' do you use?
items.correct: Sie (always capitalised, formal singular and plural).
items.why: Students default to du. German has THREE 'you' forms: du (informal singular), ihr (informal plural), Sie (formal singular OR plural - capitalised). Using du with a stranger or older person is rude. Sie takes third-person plural verb forms.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to Berlin' (by train) in German?
items.why: Students use gehen for any 'going'. In German, gehen = go ON FOOT only. FAHREN = go by vehicle (car, train, bus). FLIEGEN = go by plane. To Berlin by train: ich fahre. Saying 'ich gehe nach Berlin' suggests walking there!

items.q: How do you say 'I'm travelling to Germany' in German?
items.why: Students use 'in' or 'zu'. For most countries and cities: NACH (Deutschland, Berlin, Paris). For countries with articles (die Schweiz, die USA): IN with accusative. Easy to mix up - nach is for proper place names without articles.

items.q: What is the gender of 'Lieblingsfach' (favourite subject)?
items.why: Students think the first word determines gender. In compound nouns, the LAST element determines the gender. Fach is neuter, so das Lieblingsfach. Same rule: das Lieblingsbuch (das Buch), die Lieblingsfarbe (die Farbe).

items.q: How do you say 'I like to play football' in German?
items.why: Students translate 'like to do' word-for-word. German uses GERN (an adverb meaning 'gladly') after the verb: 'ich spiele gern' = I like playing. To intensify: 'sehr gern' (love doing) or 'lieber' (prefer doing).

items.q: How do you say 'I use my phone every day' in German?
items.correct: Ich benutze mein Handy jeden Tag.
items.misconception: Ich nutze mein Telefon jeden Tag.
items.random_wrong: Ich gebrauche mein Phone jeden Tag.
items.why: Students use 'Telefon' (which means landline/telephone). 'Handy' is the German word for mobile phone - confusingly looks like an English adjective but is the standard term. Also: BENUTZEN is the most common 'to use'.

items.q: What is the German word for 'Christmas'?
items.why: Students struggle with Weihnachten spelling - the 'ei' and 'ch' and double 'a' confuse. Also it grammatically behaves as a plural noun (zu Weihnachten = at Christmas). Other festival terms: Ostern (Easter), Silvester (New Year's Eve).

items.q: How do you say 'I drove to Munich' in the perfect tense?
items.why: Students use haben. Verbs of MOTION (fahren, gehen, kommen, fliegen, reisen) and CHANGE OF STATE take SEIN in the perfect. Critical for holiday vocab. 'Ich bin gegangen', 'ich bin geflogen', 'ich bin gefahren'.

items.q: What is the difference between 'ich will' and 'ich möchte'?
items.correct: Ich will = I want (strong, direct); ich möchte = I would like (polite).
items.why: Students learn wollen as 'to want' and use it casually. 'Ich will...' sounds DEMANDING/childish. Polite German uses ICH MÖCHTE (Konjunktiv form of mögen) for 'I would like'. Essential for job/interview contexts.

items.q: What does 'bekommen' mean in German?
items.why: A devastating faux ami. Bekommen looks like 'become' but means RECEIVE. 'Ich bekomme ein Geschenk' = I receive a present. To become = WERDEN. Confusion: 'I became a present' instead of 'I received a present'.

items.q: How do you say 'I like school because the teachers are nice' in German?
items.correct: Ich mag die Schule, weil die Lehrer nett sind.
items.misconception: Ich mag die Schule, weil die Lehrer sind nett.
items.random_wrong: Ich mag die Schule, weil sind die Lehrer nett.
items.why: Classic subordinate clause error. After WEIL, DASS, WENN, OB, the verb goes to the END of the clause. 'Weil die Lehrer nett SIND' - sind moves to the end. This rule is extensively tested.

items.q: How do you say 'my sister' in German?
items.why: Students forget possessive endings. Possessives (mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer) take the SAME ENDINGS as ein/eine. Schwester is feminine = meine. Mein Bruder (masc), meine Schwester (fem), mein Kind (neut), meine Eltern (pl).

items.q: How do you say 'I watch TV every evening' in German?
items.why: Students keep separable verbs together. FERNSEHEN splits in main clauses: sehe...fern. The prefix 'fern' goes to the END of the clause. Same with anrufen (rufe...an), aufstehen (stehe...auf), einkaufen (kaufe...ein).

items.q: When describing a past holiday in writing, which past tense is more common in German?
items.correct: Perfect tense (ich bin gefahren) for most verbs, but PRETERITE for sein, haben, modal verbs (war, hatte, konnte).
items.misconception: Always use the preterite like English simple past.
items.random_wrong: Always use present tense and add a time word.
items.why: Students apply English narrative patterns. German speech uses PERFECT for most verbs but PRETERITE for sein/haben/modals (es WAR schön, ich HATTE Spaß, ich KONNTE schwimmen). Mixing the two patterns naturally is high-level.
